title: Understand: First Amendment: Assembly and Petition
description: Check your understanding of the Assembly and Petition Clauses of the First Amendment with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Understand: First Amendment: Assembly and Petition

assessment_items.question_content:
**In *De Jonge v. Oregon,* the Court ruled that the right to assemble \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: applied to state governments as well
was protected only at the federal level
was not protected by the First Amendment
could be limited for the safety of others

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are “time, place, and manner” regulations in the context of the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Restrictions that the government can place on expressive activity like assembly and speech
Ways in which the federal government can alter state election rules
Constitutional ways in which each branch of the federal government can check the powers of the other branches
Sets of requirements issued by federal agencies to implement federal laws

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following must be true for a “time, place, and manner” restriction to be constitutionally permissible? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The restriction must be content neutral and apply to all groups.
A majority of the electorate must vote in favor of the restriction.
The president must set the restriction through an executive order.
Congress must set the restriction by passing a law.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the goal of Prince Hall’s petition?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To end slavery
To declare independence
To protect religious freedom
To secure voting rights

assessment_items.question_content:
**In which of these situations would it be constitutional to limit the right to assemble?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: If the assembly would lead to imminent lawless action
If the assembly was of a political nature
If the assembly was of a religious nature
If the assembly’s cause was not supported by most people

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these is an example of a “time, place, and manner” restriction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Requiring a group to secure a permit before holding a parade
Restricting particular groups from protesting because their views are unpopular
Requiring protesters to get state approval before holding a demonstration
Banning loud noise at night in a residential neighborhood

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the freedom of assembly protected by the First Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The freedom to gather in groups either publicly or privately
The freedom to replace the government if it does not protect people’s rights
The freedom to express one’s grievances with the government
The freedom to express one’s individual views and beliefs either publicly or privately

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the right to petition?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to ask the government to act to address a need or a wrong
The right to replace the government if it fails to protect civil rights
The freedom to practice any religious beliefs one might have
The freedom to gather in groups either publicly or privately

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes how colonial petitions were dealt with by the British government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They were often ignored, leading to increased tensions between the colonists and the British empire.
They were often received well and led to substantial changes in how the British dealt with colonial governments.
They received mixed reactions from the British monarchy, but these reasonable reactions reduced colonists’ discontent.
They led the British government to disband colonial governments and replace them with fully Loyalist governments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to constitutional scholar Daniel Carpenter, the Massachusetts General Court held what belief about the right to petition?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: All subjects, including non-citizens, could petition the government.
The right to petition was tied directly to one’s right to vote.
The government could place reasonable restrictions on the right to petition.
There was no inherent right to petition the government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which groups relied on the right to petition?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women’s suffrage movement
Abolition movement
Native Americans
Labor movement

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which TWO laws led to the decline in legislative petitions in the mid-1900s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Legislative Reorganization Act
The Administrative Procedures Act
The Civil Rights Act of 1957
The Voting Rights Act of 1965
